equated (note the erratum in 2016). Overall, it's clear that going to failure does not
benefit strength development. The only question is how close to failure is too close.

There are several reasons why training to failure may not benefit strength development
and could even harm it.
1. Once maximal motor unit recruitment is reached and muscle activity levels are
near-maximal, additional reps may not contribute an additional stimulus to
increase neural efficiency.
2. Neuromuscular fatigue can reduce motor unit firing rates and muscle activity
levels, as you've learned. For strength, high muscle activity can be more
important than total training tonnage.
3. Failed reps inherently have poor technique, which may interfere with
consolidating the optimal motor pattern in the brain.

Unlike the 2015 meta-analysis, some of the 2021 and 2022 meta-analyses found that
training to failure is not just unnecessary but actually harmful in practice, so it's worth
looking at the studies that have been published in the interim to see if training to failure


--- Page 46 ---
really is likely harmful. If you're not interested in the study details, you can skip to the
conclusion at the end.
• Fisher et al. (2015) compared 5x5 at 80% of 1RM to getting 25 reps in as few
sets as possible in untrained older individuals. Training to failure resulted in
higher perceived exertion but not significantly greater strength development,
although the effect sizes favored training to failure for MVIC torque (2 vs. 1.7).
• Pareja-Blanco et al. (2016) found training to a 15% velocity loss resulted in
similar strength development as to a 30% velocity loss in professional soccer
players, but the results trended in favor of not training as close to failure.
• Helms et al. (2018) found similar strength development when training 4-5 reps
away from failure as when training 1-3 reps away from failure. However, in
absolute terms, the group training closer to failure achieved non-significantly
greater strength gains and this study compared RPE vs. percentage-based
loading, so it wasn't primarily designed to investigate proximity to failure per se.
• Vieira et al. (2019) found no effects of training to failure on strength development.
Trained men performed a program with 3 sets of 10 reps per exercise either to
concentric muscle failure or 1-3 reps to failure for 8 weeks.
• Galiano et al. (2020) found stopping sets after a 15% velocity loss didn't
stimulate significantly greater strength gains than stopping after a 5% velocity
loss in recreationally trained men, though non-significantly it did.
• Sánchez-Moreno et al. (2020) found greater significantly 1RM strength
development during pull-ups when trained men stopped their sets after a 25%
velocity loss compared to a group stopping after a 50% velocity loss, but it was
mainly because the 25% group didn't gain a significant amount of strength to
begin with.
• Santanielo et al. (2020) compared the effect of training to failure vs. 'volitional
non-failure' in the opposite leg of trained men, resulting in doing an average of
1.6 fewer reps per set and a 11.5% lower total volume load for the non-failure
leg. The legs training to failure did not gain significantly more strength on either
exercise, nor did they gain more muscle mass (effect sizes were almost


--- Page 47 ---
identical). A few confounders in this study were the lack of standardization of
proximity to failure and a lack of standardized training volume (20% above what
they were used to). Training volume on average was quite high at ~23 sets per
week for the quads. It's possible the combination of increasing volume and
training to failure was too much at the same time.
• Bergamasco et al. (2020) found no differences in strength development or
muscle growth in untrained elderly individuals training either to failure, to
volitional interruption or even a measly 3 sets of 10 reps, all performed at 40% of
1RM. The latter finding clearly demonstrates the individuals reached a very low
ceiling effect on their gains, but at least the study provides evidence against the
idea that training to failure hurts strength development.
• Carroll et al. (2018, 2019) provide evidence against training to failure for
advanced trainees. In a study resembling the training practices of competitive
American football athletes, taking the last set of each exercise to failure led to
worse overall strength development and even a trend for reduced muscle growth
compared to training at 65% to 92.5% of maximal effort, despite obviously
greater RPEs in the group training to failure.
• Andersen et al. (2021) found no differences for muscle growth or strength
development between training to a 15% or a 30% velocity loss in volume-
matched training programs. This study had a strong design in that it used a
within-subject design in trained individuals, but they only performed leg
extensions and leg presses, so the results might differ for free weight exercises
like squats.

Overall, the negative effects of training to failure are certainly not consistent. Most
studies find no significant effects and multiple studies find a trend for greater gains
when training to failure, or at the very least greater time-efficiency. Moreover, in line with
having to achieve full motor unit recruitment and high muscle activity, other research
does point to benefits of at least going close to failure.
1. Goto et al. (2005) found that taking a 30-second break in the middle of your sets
hampers muscle growth compared to doing the same amount of reps in one


--- Page 48 ---
uninterrupted set. On the one hand, this result is extremely unsurprising.
Shocker: you have to train with some effort. On the other hand, this study was
the first proof of principle that total tonnage and repetition volume are not all that
matter. Reps performed closer to failure more effectively stimulate muscle growth
by virtue of achieving higher levels of motor unit recruitment, thereby stimulating
more mechanical tension.
2. Lim et al. (2019) confirmed that you can't rival the muscle growth of training to
failure with very submaximal training even when the total training work done is
the same.
3. More interestingly, Pareja-Blanco et al. (2017) found training to a 40% loss of
velocity in each set, which amounted to muscle failure or close to it, resulted in
greater muscle growth than stopping each set after a 20% loss of velocity: 7.7%
growth in the quads with 40% velocity loss compared to just 4.5% with 20%
velocity loss.
However, the 20% velocity loss group had significantly greater improvements in
vertical jump height and gained a similar amount of strength on the squat,
indicating training close to failure is more advantageous for muscle growth than
strength development.
4. Stragier et al. (2019) found performing 8 sets of 6 reps with ~70% of 1RM led to
inferior strength development and muscle growth than a similar workload with the
same intensity that had the participants come closer to failure (a pyramid model
of 10 sets of 3-7 reps).
5. Drinkwater et al. (2005) compared 4 sets of 6 reps at ~6RM to 8 sets of 3 reps
performed at the same intensity and in the same amount of time. Despite the
same total training volume being done, the 4x6 group tended to fail the last rep of
the session, whereas the 8x3 group rarely ever failed a rep. The 4x6 group also
experienced greater acute neuromuscular fatigue, as measured by reduced
power output post-workout. Over the course of the training program the 4x6
group gained significantly more strength on the bench press.


--- Page 49 ---
6. Karsten et al. (2019) showed that 4 sets of 10 reps with 2-minute rest intervals
lead to more growth than 8 sets of 5 reps with a 1-minute rest interval. Even
though the volume and time were equalized, presumably the metabolic stress
from the 4x10 group enhanced mechanical tension.

Thus, any negative effects of training to failure on strength development seem to occur
in the very last rep(s) when the stimulus-to-fatigue ratio becomes net detrimental.

Summary of the effects of training to failure
For muscle growth, training to failure is only beneficial in so far as it increases total
repetition volume. Reaching momentary muscle failure itself is not a growth stimulus per
se and induces a disproportionate amount of neuromuscular fatigue. Any small benefits
of training to complete failure are generally not worth the significantly greater
neuromuscular fatigue, loss of subsequent work capacity, prolonged recovery time,
potential suppression of anabolic hormone levels and increase in cortisol and blood
pressure. Thus, it's most efficient to do as many reps as you can every set but not
intentionally fail any reps. Still, it's better to train 1-2 reps close to failure and
occasionally accidentally fail a rep than to stay 3+ reps away from failure and never fail
any reps, at least if recovery is not a major limitation. As a general rule, we can also
estimate that if you're not coming within 5 reps of failure, the set can hardly be called an
effective work set at all for trained individuals looking to maximize muscle growth.

For strength development, training to failure seems to offer no benefits and may even
be harmful, because it lowers the quality of the work you do, in particular the muscle
activity, performance and technique of subsequent sets. It's best to stay 1-5 reps away
from failure. Training 1 rep to failure is a good default strategy for combined size and
strength, but if strength is more important to you than muscle growth, you can stay up to
5 reps away from failure.


--- Page 50 ---
Practically the only scenario in which training to complete failure is advisable is when
learning how to estimate your proximity to failure, which brings us to the next question.

Practical implementation: how do you judge proximity to
failure?
Not going to failure begs the question of how you determine how close you are to
momentary muscle failure in the first place. There are several methods to measure this.

Velocity-based training
One way to determine how close you are to reaching muscular failure is by assessing
your movement speed, like the upward velocity of the barbell during a squat. As you
come closer to failing a repetition in a set of any exercise, your movement speed will
decrease. As you reach failure, the movement speed becomes zero.

As such, movement velocity can serve as a measure of proximity to muscle failure.
When you know your movement velocity at specific repetition maximums for an
exercise, your velocity loss across a set can extremely accurately determine how many
reps you could still perform if you went to failure.

Some research suggests velocity-based training can improve strength development.
Shattock & Tee (2022) found that rugby players training until a prescribed velocity loss
achieved considerably greater strength and power development after 6 weeks than a
group training until a subjectively estimated level of fatigue (RPE/RIR, discussed
below). Both groups achieved the same total training work volume and trained with the
same intended intensities (%1RM), so objectively, the stimulus should have been
similar, yet the velocity-based group achieved better performance gains. How? Velocity-
based training has the major advantage that it's objective, so it probably aligns our
training stimulus more closely with our true level of neuromuscular fatigue. Maybe you
thought you could do more, but you were pushing too close to failure. Alternatively,


--- Page 51 ---
maybe you told yourself you were close enough to failure, but the objective data
showed you could push harder.

Moreover, acute feedback on your movement velocity may be motivational and
educational to train as explosively as possible. As discussed in the course module on
rep tempo, training with explosive concentrics can improve force and rate of force
development.

While velocity-based training is great in theory, the relation between number of possible
repetitions left and velocity varies significantly per individual, load and exercise [2], so
you can't accurately determine how many repetitions someone could still do if you don't
have prior data about their movement velocity for that exercise at that percentage of
1RM. Generalized algorithms aren't very useful.

Thus, to implement velocity-guided training optimally, you need to personally monitor
your movement velocity for each exercise. This is much easier to do in a laboratory than
in a commercial gym. While there are some commercially available and practical apps
and sensors that claim to function as reliable accelerometers, few have been validated
in research and even if you have a good kit, it's a lot of work to set up. The table below
summarizes the steps you'd need to take for each exercise to determine its load-
velocity profile, along with a sample load-velocity profile. These data could then be used
to estimate 1RM and training intensities based on current training velocity.


--- Page 52 ---
How to construct an exercise's load velocity-profile. Source: this is a great and freely
available paper on how to implement velocity-based training if you're up for the work.

If you don't have a validated sensor and app, the common implementation of velocity-
based training is something along the lines of: "You stop the set when your
explosiveness decreases/movement speed decreases/your reps start grinding." Such
instructions are barely more objective than estimating reps to failure. Some people have
the idea that your movement velocity suddenly drops at some level of fatigue; in reality,


--- Page 53 ---
movement speed already starts decreasing very far away from muscular failure and
continues to decrease gradually. At which point do you determine the decrease is
significant or notable? You may think you can do this, but look at the actual velocity
measurements during a 6RM bench press in the graph below. There is no clear cut-off
point in either average or peak speed of movement, not in the upward phase of the
movement nor in the downward phase.

Source

Based on objective data, you should consider a decrease in movement velocity more
like a minimum level of training intensiveness than a maximum, at least for muscle
growth. Using subjectively perceived movement velocity as a measure of closeness to


--- Page 54 ---
momentary muscle failure in a set is at best a very rough measure that requires an
arbitrarily determined cut-off point of how much speed reduction is acceptable.

So is it worth implement velocity-based training and getting the required equipment for
it? Probably not. A 2022 meta-analysis of the literature by Orange et al. found that
velocity-based training did not result in better strength development than other methods
to estimate proximity to failure.

Still, in practice it's very useful to think of velocity loss during a set as a measure of
proximity to failure. True momentary muscle failure is preceded by significant velocity
loss, until the movement comes to a complete halt in the biomechanical sticking point. If
someone fails a rep at any other point during the exercise, the failure was either due to
suboptimal exercise technique or lack of effort, not true momentary muscle failure.
Similarly, if somebody stops a set before there's any measurable velocity loss, they
most likely weren't close to momentary muscle failure and the failure was thus
psychological: this is often called 'volitional failure'. Unfortunately, even many
researchers do not properly distinguish between momentary muscle failure ('true
failure') and volitional failure (lack of effort).

RPEs
Ratings of perceived exertion (RPE) are, as the name implies, a rating on a scale (say
1-10) of how difficult something is. A 10/10 RPE is training to failure by definition, so the
RPE score can serve as a measure of how close you are to that.

There are various definitions and scales of RPE, but there's no need to go into them
individually, because you probably shouldn't use any of them. The validity of RPE as a
measure of training intensiveness is fundamentally flawed, because it is based on the
incorrect underlying belief that your subjective perception and judgement of your effort
are a reliable indication of your objective performance potential. Any subjective measure
is by definition specific to the individual. For example, what level of pain is cause for


--- Page 55 ---
concern? If you just ask "Does it hurt?" some people may whine it's intolerable at the
earliest signs of discomfort, whereas another person may be walking around with bone
protruding through the skin and reassure you "It's just a flesh wound." In that same vein,
albeit less extreme, bodybuilders report lower levels of RPE at the exact same
intensiveness of intensity × reps than recreationally active individuals. Several other
variables can influence our performance and RPE differentially.
• The presence of a spotter can decrease RPE while increasing the number of
bench press reps performed by increasing your self-efficacy ('confidence').
• Oxygen shortage can increase RPE without affecting performance.
• Music can considerably affect the perception of effort while having minimal
impact on objective performance [2].
• Transcranial magnetic stimulation - buzzing our brain with a targeted electrical
current - can increase repetition performance without affecting our RPE.

Most proponents of the use of RPEs have come to the same conclusion: over time,
most coaches' definition of RPE has shifted further away from subjective effort and
closer to being a needlessly complex version of repetitions to failure (RTF). RPE is now
often defined as the inverse of RTF and given the new name of Repetitions in Reserve,
which is essentially the same as RTF.

Reps to failure
The simplest and most practical way to determine proximity to failure is by stating it in
terms of the amount of repetitions you're away from reaching momentary muscle failure:
reps to failure (RTF). Many researchers use a similar scale called Reps in Reserve
(RIR), but the disadvantage of this scale is that it doesn't include muscular failure, as 0
RIR could mean either failure or that you think your next rep would fail.

With RTF, the scale logically goes from failure upward:
0 RTF means your last rep of the set fails despite maximum effort: you hit momentary
concentric muscle failure.


--- Page 56 ---
1 RTF means you perform every rep you can except the one that you think would fail.
2 RTF means you leave 1 rep in the tank.
3 RTF means you leave 2 reps in the tank.
Etc.

Estimated reps to failure more accurately predict actual reps to failure than RPE scores
do. Trained individuals are typically reasonably accurate at estimating how many reps
away from failure they are and they can become even more accurate with practice,
although a 2021 meta-analysis found trained individuals are actually no more accurate
on average than untrained individuals at estimating reps to failure and stronger
individuals also don't seem to be better at estimating their RTF. Nevertheless, a
motivated and trained individual's estimated RTF is typically within 1 rep of actual reps
to failure. However, most people, especially lesser trained or motivated individuals, still
underestimate how many reps they can do by up to a few reps and the more tired
people get, the more prone they are to underestimate how many reps they still had left
to failure. Very few people accidentally hit momentary muscle failure. During particularly
effortful exercises like squats, many people, probably most people, never accidentally
hit failure in their entire life. People generally become less accurate in their predictions
during higher-rep sets and when they stay further away from failure [2], which is logical,
because a given error percentage constitutes a higher number of reps for higher
estimates. If you generally underestimate how many reps you could still do by a quarter,
that's not even a full rep for your 3RM, but it's 5 reps for your 20RM.

Conclusion
To induce maximal muscle growth, training one rep to failure (1 RTF) is often advisable.
Every rep contributes to muscle growth, so it's most efficient to do all the reps you can.
However, try not to regularly fail any reps, as it has a poor stimulus-to-fatigue ratio and it
limits your subsequent work capacity.


--- Page 57 ---
1-5 RTF is all you need for maximum strength development in the short term, but the
advantage of doing every rep you can is that you can more accurately track your
progression. The accuracy of the reps-to-failure estimation decreases the more
submaximal you train. What seems like strength progression may just be training closer
to failure. Plus, in the long term, muscle growth should increase strength, so training 1
RTF is an effective and practical rule of thumb for most lifters. However, powerlifters
and athletes that care more about strength than muscle growth - especially those
restricted by weight classes - probably achieve an optimal stimulus-to-fatigue ratio
around 2-3 RTF. This roughly means you do as many high-quality reps as you can, but
you don't squeeze out the last few reps that would require you to grind with potentially
shaky technique.

While you ideally shouldn't regularly train to failure regardless of goal, accidentally
hitting failure occasionally can be fine, especially when training for muscle growth. It's a
sign you're training hard enough. If you never accidentally hit muscle failure when trying
to stay 1 rep to failure, your training efforts are questionable and it's advisable to
occasionally take the last set of an exercise to failure, provided this is safely possible.
Many trainees do not go nearly 1 rep to failure unless pushed by someone else. As
such, for not-so-motivated clients, you may simply want to tell them to train to complete
failure, as they probably won't do so anyway. Adding the complexity of RTF scales and
fear of failure may only decrease training efforts, so a simple 'train to failure' instruction
may be best in such cases.


--- Page 58 ---
Breathing
How should you breathe during exercise? A study by Blazek et al. (2021) compared
how multiple breathing techniques affect 1RM bench press strength:
- The Valsalva maneuver (VM): breathing in deeply while lowering the weight and then
exhaling against a closed glottis while pushing. In other words, you keep the air tight
inside you until you're past the hardest part of the lift.
- Holding your breath: similar to the VM but without inhaling extra air before you stop
exhaling.
- Lung packing (PAC): this is like an ultra-VM where you swallow in extra air multiple
times to really stuff your lungs before the lift.
- Reverse breathing (REVB): breathing in when pushing up the weight and out when
lowering the weight.

The general trend was that reverse breathing decreased performance, while all the
techniques that involved holding your breath during the push were effective. This means
we intuitively tend to breathe properly.

The Valsalva maneuver and lung packing showed a trend to make the lift the most
effortless: they decreased the time spent in the sticking region. These techniques
increase intra-abdominal pressure by making us hold extra air in our lungs and keeping
that pressure inside us until we're past the hardest part of the lift.

In conclusion, most people breathe best by simply not thinking about how to breathe.
You'll do it right intuitively. However, for extra hard reps, especially during squats and
deadlifts, you should breathe in some extra air to your core extra tight before the rep
and stay tight until you're past the sticking point.
